name: Ranzha
description: The Ranzha method is an intermediate Skewb speedsolving technique created by Brandon Harnish. It emphasizes intuition-based solving rather than heavy algorithm reliance, making it a creative and flexible approach. The method builds on concepts from Sarah's method family and offers a balance between algorithmic and intuitive solving.

steps.name: First Face
steps.description: Solve a complete face of the Skewb intuitively. This establishes the base layer and provides reference points for the remaining solve.
tips: Plan the first face during inspection for a fast start
Be color-neutral to find the easiest face to solve
Use efficient insertions — avoid unnecessary moves

steps.name: Opposite Face Corners
steps.description: Solve or orient the corners of the opposite face using intuitive techniques. This step brings the top-layer corners into their correct positions relative to the solved first face.
tips: Use intuitive understanding of how Skewb moves affect opposite-layer corners
Sledgehammer and anti-sledgehammer sequences are useful here
Predict the state of these corners during the first face step

steps.name: Finish Centers and Alignment
steps.description: Complete the solve by aligning all remaining centers. This may involve short algorithmic sequences from the Sarah family or intuitive adjustments.
tips: Familiarity with Sarah's algorithms speeds up this step
Some finishes can be done purely intuitively
Practice recognizing the center configurations quickly

pros: Encourages intuitive understanding of the Skewb
Flexible solving approach with room for creativity
Builds naturally on Sarah method knowledge
Good balance of speed and low algorithm count
cons: Less structured than purely algorithmic methods
May not be consistent enough for top-level competition
creator: Brandon Harnish
